Even when there is nothing to clean up this is an extra DB write round-trip on a read path. With a 7-day window, the cleanup result is empty on ~99% of calls (the average user does not complete 20 todos per week and immediately check again). The semantic of 'list my todos' causing side effects also makes the endpoint non-idempotent.
findings.root_cause: Cleanup was embedded in the read path as a convenience so stale todos are always cleared before presenting the list, avoiding a separate cron or scheduled task.
findings.impact: One extra DB round-trip per list call per user. With frequent frontend polling (e.g. dashboard refresh every 30s) this doubles the DB query load from this endpoint. At scale (100 concurrent users) this generates ~200 DB queries/min from a single widget. Severity is limited by the hard 20-item cap on todos — the deleteMany always scans at most 20 rows.
findings.suggested_fix: Move cleanup to a NestJS `@Cron` job. If a synchronous side effect is preferred, at minimum add a cheap count-before-delete check: skip the DELETE if `personalTodo.count({ where: { userId, completed: true, completedAt: { lt: cutoff } } }) === 0`.
acceptance_criteria: GET /personal-todos does not issue a DELETE when no completed todos are older than 7 days
Stale completed todos are still cleaned up (via cron or lazy count-gated delete)
findings.notes: Verbatim confirmed. `cleanupOldCompleted` at lines 102-115 issues an unconditional `deleteMany` with no guard. Minor severity given the 20-item hard cap on personal todos — the deleteMany will always scan at most 20 rows. Still a semantic issue and unnecessary write latency.

findings.title: findAssignments (predefined-tasks): no pagination and no maximum result cap
findings.description: The `findAssignments` method has no `take` limit. A request with `?startDate=2020-01-01&endDate=2030-01-01` returns all assignments in a 10-year window, potentially thousands of rows with included relations.
findings.root_cause: No `take` argument on the unbounded `findMany`.
findings.impact: Wide date-range queries return unbounded result sets with includes (predefinedTask with multiple fields, user, assignedBy). Memory and response-size risk for large data sets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a reasonable default cap: `take: Math.min(filters.limit ?? 500, 1000)`. Alternatively add pagination params to the query DTO.
acceptance_criteria: GET /predefined-tasks/assignments with wide date range returns at most 1000 rows

findings.title: findRecurringRules: no pagination — unbounded findMany with includes
findings.description: The method returns all active recurring rules matching the optional filters, with no `take` or pagination. Includes nested predefinedTask, user, and createdBy.
findings.root_cause: No `take` argument on the findMany.
findings.impact: If many users have many recurring rules (e.g. 100 users × 5 rules each = 500 rows) and no filter is applied, the full set is returned in one response with nested includes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `take: 500` as a hard cap or accept pagination params.
acceptance_criteria: GET /predefined-tasks/recurring-rules returns at most 500 rows

findings.title: captureSnapshots fetches ALL active projects with full task/milestone arrays — no pagination
findings.description: `captureSnapshots` loads ALL active projects at once, each with their full task and milestone arrays. For 500 active projects with an average of 200 tasks and 20 milestones each, this loads 100,000 task rows and 10,000 milestone rows in a single query. The comment credits this as a 2-query optimization (PER-003), which is correct vs. the N+1 it replaced, but the in-memory data volume is unbounded.
findings.root_cause: The snapshot cron job was optimized from N+1 to 2 queries, but the first query has no `take` ceiling, so the entire active-project table is hydrated in one shot.
findings.impact: On large deployments, the snapshot job can transiently consume hundreds of MB of Node.js heap while processing the full result set. If run frequently, this creates GC pressure.
findings.suggested_fix: Process snapshots in batches (e.g., 100 projects at a time) using cursor-based pagination: `findMany({ where: { status: 'ACTIVE', id: { gt: cursor } }, take: 100, orderBy: { id: 'asc' } })`. Wrap each batch in its own `createMany`. For very large deployments, replace the in-memory task/milestone array with a `task.groupBy` per batch.
acceptance_criteria: captureSnapshots processes at most 100 projects per DB round-trip
All active projects still receive a snapshot after the refactor
findings.notes: For the current scale of use (local government, likely < 200 active projects) this is a nit. It becomes blocking above ~500 active projects. Confirmed verbatim at lines 1145-1152.

findings.title: findAll school-vacations: unbounded findMany — no pagination for year-scoped list
findings.description: GET /school-vacations with no `year` filter returns all school vacation records across all years. The table grows with each import cycle (each zone × year adds up to ~10 rows). Without a limit, an organisation with several years of data will return all records unboundedly.
findings.root_cause: No `take` clause; the `year` filter is optional with no fallback cap.
findings.impact: Low current impact (table stays small), but the endpoint is missing the standard defensive cap pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `take: 200` as a hard cap or require the `year` query parameter (set it as required in the controller or default to current year).
acceptance_criteria: GET /school-vacations returns at most 200 rows
findings.notes: Code evidence verified verbatim at lines 36-45 of school-vacations.service.ts.

findings.title: settings.service `initializeDefaultSettings` issues N×2 sequential DB queries at every module init
findings.description: On every module init (every API startup), the service issues one `findUnique` per default setting key, then one `create` if missing — up to 2×N sequential DB round-trips for N default settings (currently ~10). While the number of settings is small and bounded, this pattern runs on every pod restart including rolling deploys.
findings.root_cause: Sequential loop instead of a single `upsert`-all or `createMany`-on-conflict batch.
findings.impact: ~20 sequential DB queries at startup per replica. Low severity given the small and fixed N, but adds latency to rolling deploys.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the loop with `this.prisma.appSettings.createMany({ data: DEFAULT_SETTINGS entries, skipDuplicates: true })` — one round-trip regardless of N.
acceptance_criteria: Module init executes exactly 1 DB statement regardless of how many default settings exist
Default settings are still created when absent
findings.notes: Also applies to `resetAllToDefaults` at line 300 which does sequential awaited `update` calls. Verified verbatim match at lines 109-126.

findings.title: getSkillsMatrix() loads all active users and all skills without pagination — response size grows as O(users × skills)
findings.description: The skills matrix endpoint fetches ALL active users (with nested department + skills includes) and ALL skills in parallel, then cross-joins them in JavaScript. No pagination, no limit. For a large organisation (500 users × 200 skills), the JSON response encodes 500 × 200 = 100 000 cell objects. At ~100 bytes each that is ~10 MB per request. The `users` findMany also eagerly includes `skills { include: { skill: true } }` which is an O(U×S) JOIN from Postgres.
findings.root_cause: The matrix is designed as a reporting view; pagination was not considered because it is used in a grid component that requires the full dataset client-side.
findings.impact: At >200 users the response exceeds the 100 KB payload guideline. At >500 users it can push several megabytes, causing serialization spikes on the API and increasing client memory pressure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add pagination on the users axis (`page`/`limit`) with a hard cap (e.g. 200 users per page). For full-matrix exports, offer a streaming CSV endpoint. At minimum document and enforce an org-size assumption in the response type.
acceptance_criteria: GET /skills/matrix with 600 active users returns at most 200 user rows by default
A `page` parameter allows consumers to paginate through all users
findings.notes: Verbatim confirmed at lines 362-441. `whereUser` only filters `isActive: true` — no limit, no skip, no pagination parameter. The endpoint does require `skills:manage_matrix` permission (ADMIN/RESPONSABLE/MANAGER only), which partially limits blast radius, but the payload-size concern holds at org scale. Classified as nit because this endpoint is a reporting tool with restricted access.

findings.title: TeleworkSchedule: no index on (userId, date) range queries — only unique constraint
findings.description: The `TeleworkSchedule` model (schema line 733) declares `@@unique([userId, date])` but no separate `@@index([userId, date])`. Postgres creates a B-tree index on the unique constraint, which IS usable for range queries on `(userId, date)`. However, `findForPlanningOverview` queries `userId: { in: [...new Set(userIds)] }, date: { gte, lte }` — an IN list on userId with a date range. The unique constraint index on `(userId, date)` would be used for single-user lookups, but for multi-userId IN list queries Postgres may choose a bitmap index scan or sequential scan instead depending on cardinality. The schema has no composite index optimised for this access pattern.
findings.root_cause: The `@@unique` serves integrity, not query performance for multi-user range scans; no dedicated range index exists.
findings.impact: Medium: `findForPlanningOverview` with many userIds (planning view for 30+ users) may perform poorly without an index on (date, userId) that favours the date-range-first access pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([date, userId])` to `TeleworkSchedule` to complement the unique constraint, optimising the date-range-first queries used in planning overview.
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN ANALYZE on `SELECT * FROM telework_schedules WHERE user_id IN (...) AND date BETWEEN ? AND ?` shows an Index Scan not a Seq Scan for tables with > 10k rows
findings.notes: Schema confirmed: only @@unique([userId, date]) at line 733, no @@index([date, userId]). The Postgres query planner may already use the unique index effectively for small tables; this finding applies at scale. Verified code evidence at lines 431-449 of telework.service.ts.

findings.title: findAll time-tracking: effective cap is 1000 rows — far exceeds recommended 100
findings.code_evidence: const safeLimit = Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000);
findings.description: The time-tracking `findAll` method allows callers to retrieve up to 1000 time entries per page. Each entry includes user, thirdParty, declaredBy, task, and project joins. At 1000 rows with 5 joins, the serialised payload can approach 500 KB for entries with verbose descriptions.
findings.root_cause: No documented rationale for the 1000-row cap; likely carried over from an earlier iteration without review.
findings.impact: A MANAGER calling GET /time-tracking without date filters for a large project can receive a multi-hundred-KB response that degrades both server and client performance.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower the default to 50 and cap to 100, consistent with best practice.
acceptance_criteria: GET /time-tracking with no limit returns at most 50 rows
GET /time-tracking?limit=2000 returns at most 100 rows
findings.notes: Code evidence verified verbatim at line 363 of time-tracking.service.ts.

findings.title: validateImport fetches ALL users (email+login) for duplicate detection — unbounded full-table scan
findings.description: `validateImport` pre-fetches every user row (email + login) into memory to build Sets for duplicate detection. On a deployment with 10,000 users this loads 10,000 rows per dry-run validation call. While this is better than per-row queries (as done by `importUsers`), it does not scale. No `take` or `where` limit is applied.
findings.root_cause: The pre-fetch pattern was chosen as an optimization over per-row queries but was not bounded.
findings.impact: At 10,000+ users, the full-table scan of (email, login) adds significant data transfer on every import validation. Memory usage in the API process spikes to hold the full arrays before Set construction.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the full-table scan with targeted lookups: extract all unique emails and logins from the import batch, then query `user.findMany({ where: { OR: [{ email: { in: emails } }, { login: { in: logins } }] } })` to fetch only potential duplicates. This reduces the scan to at most `batch_size` matches rather than the full user table.
acceptance_criteria: POST /users/import/validate with 50 users makes a single bounded query (WHERE email IN (...) OR login IN (...)) instead of a full-table findMany
findings.notes: Confirmed verbatim at lines 1479-1481. No where clause or take limit present.

findings.title: All 6 pages use manual fetch + useState with no TanStack Query caching — repeated fetches on every navigation
findings.description: All 6 pages use the same pattern: `useEffect + manual fetch + useState`. None use TanStack Query which is listed in the project stack (CLAUDE.md). Every navigation to these pages triggers a full re-fetch (no staleTime, no background revalidation, no deduplication). The leaves page fires 8 concurrent requests on every mount. The CLAUDE.md convention specifies TanStack Query for frontend data fetching.
findings.root_cause: Pages were built without adopting TanStack Query, leading to manual fetch orchestration with no caching layer.
findings.impact: Unnecessary network load on every navigation; no optimistic updates possible without rollback logic; no deduplication when multiple components fetch the same data. Navigation feels slower than needed. This is nit severity as it's a design debt issue rather than a bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Migrate to `useQuery` / `useMutation` from TanStack Query. Define query keys per domain (e.g., `['leaves', 'mine']`, `['leaves', 'pending']`). Set `staleTime: 30_000` for relatively static data like leave types and balances. Use `invalidateQueries` after mutations instead of calling `fetchAll()`.
acceptance_criteria: Each page uses useQuery for data fetching.
Navigating away and back does not trigger a loading spinner if data is fresh.
Mutations use useMutation and invalidate relevant query keys.
findings.notes: Confirmed: grep for useQuery/useMutation returns 0 matches in leaves/page.tsx and events/page.tsx. Nit severity because it is well-known architectural debt requiring significant refactoring.

findings.title: planning/page.tsx is entirely a shell that could be an RSC with a single client import
findings.description: The planning page is marked `"use client"` but does not use any client-only hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef, etc.) itself. It is a pure pass-through shell around a dynamically imported component. If `MainLayout` is also an RSC (or can be made one), this page does not need to be a Client Component — removing `"use client"` from the shell would allow Next.js to server-render the outer layout and only hydrate PlanningView on the client, reducing the client JS bundle for the shell.
findings.root_cause: The `"use client"` directive was added prophylactically or because the developer was unsure if it was needed.
findings.impact: Minor: the page shell is included in the client JS bundle unnecessarily. This increases Time To Interactive slightly for users on slow connections.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `"use client"` from `planning/page.tsx`. If `MainLayout` requires client context, ensure PlanningView is imported with `dynamic` and `ssr: false` (already done). Verify the build still works and the page renders correctly.
acceptance_criteria: Removing `"use client"` from planning/page.tsx does not cause a build error
PlanningPage renders correctly in production build
findings.notes: Adversarial check: MainLayout is itself a client component ('use client' + useState + usePathname). In Next.js App Router, a Server Component CAN import and render a Client Component — planning/page.tsx does not need 'use client' just because MainLayout uses it. The finding is valid. Confidence kept at medium since this requires a build-verified check before applying.

findings.title: PredefinedTaskAssignment has no index on predefinedTaskId — per-task assignment lookups may seq-scan
findings.code_evidence:
model PredefinedTaskAssignment {
  id                  String    @id @default(uuid())
  predefinedTaskId    String
  userId              String
  date                DateTime
  period              DayPeriod // DAT-012: was String "MORNING"|"AFTERNOON"|"FULL_DAY"
  assignedById        String?
  isRecurring         Boolean   @default(false)
  recurringRuleId     String?   
  completionStatus    AssignmentCompletionStatus @default(NOT_DONE) // DAT-012: was String
  completedAt         DateTime?
  completedById       String?   
  notApplicableReason String?
  createdAt           DateTime  @default(now())
  updatedAt           DateTime  @updatedAt

  @@unique([predefinedTaskId, userId, date, period])
  @@index([date, userId])
  @@index([completionStatus, date])
findings.description: The unique constraint on `(predefinedTaskId, userId, date, period)` has `predefinedTaskId` as the leading column, so per-task queries (`WHERE predefinedTaskId = X`) use this unique index. However, queries on `(userId)` alone (which is the second column of the unique) cannot use the unique index efficiently. The `findAssignments` method in the service filters by `userId` and optionally `predefinedTaskId` and `date`; when only `userId` is supplied, the `[date, userId]` composite index can be used (userId is the trailing column), but filtering by `userId` only (no date) would require a full scan.
findings.root_cause: No dedicated `@@index([userId])` for the `predefinedTaskId` + `userId` filter combination when predefinedTaskId is not present.
findings.impact: When `findAssignments({ userId })` is called without `startDate`/`endDate`, the query cannot use the `[date, userId]` index efficiently because `date` is the leading column. This is a seq-scan risk as assignments grow.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([userId]) // reverse-lookup for per-user assignment queries` to `PredefinedTaskAssignment`.
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN on SELECT ... FROM predefined_task_assignments WHERE user_id = $1 shows Index Scan
findings.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres -d orchestra -c "EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM predefined_task_assignments WHERE user_id = 'test';"
findings.notes: The `[date, userId]` composite index covers the common (date-scoped) query path. This finding only applies to unbounded userId-only queries.

findings.title: getLeaveBalance: Promise.all fan-out issues N×2 DB queries, one resolveAllocatedDays per leave type
findings.description: `getLeaveBalance` loads all active leave types, then fans out into `Promise.all(leaveTypes.map(...))` where each iteration calls `resolveAllocatedDays` — which itself issues 2 sequential queries (individual balance findUnique + global balance findFirst). With N leave types (e.g. 8), this fires 16 concurrent DB queries. While concurrent rather than sequential, this adds per-request DB connection pressure and latency tail. The worker comment (PER-002) documents the bulk allLeaves approach but `resolveAllocatedDays` still fans out.
findings.root_cause: `resolveAllocatedDays` is not batch-aware: it always issues 2 queries for a single (userId, leaveTypeId, year) triple. There is no bulk variant that resolves all types for a user in a single query.
findings.impact: GET /leaves/me/balance issues ~16 DB queries per call (for 8 leave types). At high traffic, this multiplies connection pool usage by ×16 compared to a single bulk query.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `resolveAllocatedDaysForAll(userId, leaveTypeIds, year)` variant that uses `leaveBalance.findMany({ where: { userId, leaveTypeId: { in: leaveTypeIds }, year } })` and `leaveBalance.findMany({ where: { userId: null, leaveTypeId: { in: leaveTypeIds }, year } })` — two queries total, mapped to a Record<leaveTypeId, days> in memory.
acceptance_criteria: GET /leaves/me/balance issues at most 4 DB queries regardless of the number of leave types (1 allLeaves + 2 bulk balance lookups + 1 leaveTypes list)
findings.notes: Lower priority than P3-3/P3-4 because the fan-out is bounded by the number of leave types (typically < 15) and the queries run in parallel. resolveAllocatedDays verified at lines 2494-2524: exactly 2 sequential DB calls per invocation (findUnique individual + findFirst global).
